quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Converging lens (convex)"?

options.text: β = 10 log₁₀(I/I₀) in decibels (dB); I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m² is the threshold of hearing; each 10 dB = 10× intensity
Gas pressure results from molecular collisions with container walls; temperature measures average translational kinetic energy: KE_avg = (3/2)kT
f > 0; brings parallel light to a real focal point; produces real or virtual images depending on object position
P_total = P₁ + P₂; equivalent to adding the reciprocals of focal lengths

quiz_questions.explanation: "Converging lens (convex)" is defined as: f > 0; brings parallel light to a real focal point; produces real or virtual images depending on object position. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Diverging lens (concave)"?

options.text: Diagnostic imaging (gamma cameras, PET) and therapy (targeted radiation, brachytherapy) using radioactive isotopes
Central maximum is widest; minima at a sin θ = mλ (m ≠ 0); a = slit width; narrower slit → wider pattern
ᴬ_Z X: A = mass number (p + n), Z = atomic number (protons), N = A - Z = neutrons; isotopes differ in N
f < 0; causes parallel light to diverge as if from a virtual focal point; always produces virtual, upright, reduced images

quiz_questions.explanation: "Diverging lens (concave)" is defined as: f < 0; causes parallel light to diverge as if from a virtual focal point; always produces virtual, upright, reduced images. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Thin lens equation"?

options.text: 1/f = (n-1)(1/R₁ - 1/R₂); relates focal length to the lens material (n) and surface curvatures (R₁, R₂)
Bright colored lines on a dark background; produced by excited atoms emitting photons at characteristic frequencies
J.J. Thomson used crossed E and B fields to measure the electron's charge-to-mass ratio e/m, discovering the electron
1/d_o + 1/d_i = 1/f; same form as mirror equation; positive d_i = real image (opposite side); negative d_i = virtual image (same side)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Thin lens equation" is defined as: 1/d_o + 1/d_i = 1/f; same form as mirror equation; positive d_i = real image (opposite side); negative d_i = virtual image (same side). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Ray diagrams"?

options.text: Three principal rays: (1) parallel to axis → through F, (2) through center → straight, (3) through F → parallel to axis
Two converging lenses (objective + eyepiece); total magnification M = m_objective × M_eyepiece; enlarges nearby small objects
ε = -L(dI/dt); a changing current in a coil induces an EMF in itself; L = inductance in henries (H)
1/f = (n-1)(1/R₁ - 1/R₂); relates focal length to the lens material (n) and surface curvatures (R₁, R₂)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Ray diagrams" is defined as: Three principal rays: (1) parallel to axis → through F, (2) through center → straight, (3) through F → parallel to axis. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the context of Lenses, which best defines "Magnification"?

options.text: m = -d_i/d_o; same as for mirrors; determines image size and orientation
ε = -L(dI/dt); a changing current in a coil induces an EMF in itself; L = inductance in henries (H)
ε = BLv; induced when a conductor of length L moves with velocity v perpendicular to field B
Light nuclei combine: ²H + ³H → ⁴He + n + 17.6 MeV; requires extreme temperatures (>10⁷ K) to overcome Coulomb barrier

quiz_questions.explanation: In Lenses, "Magnification" refers to: m = -d_i/d_o; same as for mirrors; determines image size and orientation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "f > 0; brings parallel light to a real focal point; produces real or virtual images depending on object position"?

options.text: E-V relationship
Heat engine efficiency
Converging lens (convex)
Photon

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Converging lens (convex)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "f < 0; causes parallel light to diverge as if from a virtual focal point; always produces virtual, upright, reduced images"?

options.text: Thomson's experiment
Diverging lens (concave)
Malus's law
Magnetic flux

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Diverging lens (concave)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "1/d_o + 1/d_i = 1/f; same form as mirror equation; positive d_i = real image (opposite side); negative d_i = virtual image (same side)"?

options.text: Self-inductance
Thin lens equation
Quantum tunneling
Longitudinal wave

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Thin lens equation".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Three principal rays: (1) parallel to axis → through F, (2) through center → straight, (3) through F → parallel to axis"?

options.text: Ray diagrams
Dispersion
Heisenberg uncertainty principle
Pair production

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Ray diagrams".

quiz_questions.question_text: In Lenses, which term matches: "m = -d_i/d_o; same as for mirrors; determines image size and orientation"?

options.text: Convex mirror
Wave energy
Magnification
Superconductivity

quiz_questions.explanation: In Lenses, this refers to "Magnification".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Lenses is accurate?

options.text: The dielectric reduces the electric field between plates and allows more charge to be stored at the same voltage.
Superposition: the net electric field from multiple charges is the vector sum of individual fields.
Capacitors and Dielectrics store electrical energy in an electric field.
Lenses use refraction to form images.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Lenses as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Lenses?

options.text: Light as a Wave examines the wave nature of light in detail.
Radioactive decay follows N(t) = N₀e^(-λt), with half-life t₁/₂ = ln(2)/λ.
Converging (convex) lenses are thicker in the middle and bring parallel rays to a focus; diverging (concave) lenses are thinner in the middle and spread rays apart.
Capacitors and Dielectrics store electrical energy in an electric field.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Lenses as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Lenses?

options.text: The second law states that natural processes increase the total entropy of the universe; heat flows spontaneously from hot to cold, never the reverse, without external work.
Electromagnetic interactions are one of the four fundamental forces of nature.
Like mirrors, the thin lens equation 1/d_o + 1/d_i = 1/f applies (with sign conventions).
Applications of Quantum Ideas extends quantum principles to technologies and fundamental physics.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Lenses as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Lenses?

options.text: This force is short-range (~1 fm), extremely strong, and charge-independent.
Converging lenses produce real inverted images when the object is beyond f, and virtual upright magnified images when the object is inside f.
Both release energy because the products have higher binding energy per nucleon.
Key properties: wavelength (λ), frequency (f), amplitude (A), and wave speed (v = fλ).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Lenses as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Lenses?

options.text: The resolution limit of optical instruments is set by the Rayleigh criterion.
Optical Instruments combine lenses and mirrors to extend human vision.
EMF can be induced by changing B, changing A, or changing the angle θ between B and the area vector.
Diverging lenses always produce virtual, upright, reduced images.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Lenses as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Lenses?

options.text: Cameras: converging lenses focus light from objects at varying distances onto the film/sensor by adjusting the lens-to-sensor distance (focusing)
Induction motors: AC current creates rotating magnetic fields that induce currents in the rotor, producing torque—powering most industrial machinery and household appliances
MRI contrast agents: paramagnetic gadolinium compounds enhance MRI images by altering the magnetic relaxation times of nearby water molecules
Nuclear power plants: controlled fission of ²³⁵U in fuel rods heats water to produce steam that drives turbines, generating ~20% of US electricity without direct CO₂ emissions

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Lenses.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Lenses?

options.text: Car side mirrors are convex ('objects in mirror are closer than they appear') to provide a wider field of view, reducing blind spots
Corrective lenses: nearsightedness (diverging lens to push focus back) and farsightedness (converging lens to push focus forward) are corrected by choosing the appropriate focal length
MRI contrast agents: paramagnetic gadolinium compounds enhance MRI images by altering the magnetic relaxation times of nearby water molecules
Electric generators convert mechanical energy to electrical energy: rotating coils in magnetic fields produce alternating EMF (AC power) via Faraday's law

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Lenses.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Lenses applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Projectors: a converging lens creates a magnified real image of a small display on a distant screen, with the object placed just outside the focal length
Anti-reflective coatings on lenses use thin-film destructive interference: film thickness is chosen so reflected waves cancel, maximizing transmitted light
Noise barriers on highways: effective at blocking high-frequency (short λ) sound but less effective for low-frequency (long λ) sound that diffracts over the top
MRI contrast agents: paramagnetic gadolinium compounds enhance MRI images by altering the magnetic relaxation times of nearby water molecules

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Lenses.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Lenses?

options.text: CD/DVD reading: a laser reflects off pits and lands with different path lengths, creating constructive and destructive interference that encodes digital data
Carbon dating: the ratio of ¹⁴C to ¹²C in organic material decreases over time due to ¹⁴C decay; measuring this ratio determines the age of archaeological artifacts
Cameras: converging lenses focus light from objects at varying distances onto the film/sensor by adjusting the lens-to-sensor distance (focusing)
Laser systems: multi-lens arrangements expand, collimate, and focus laser beams with precise control for applications from surgery to manufacturing

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Lenses.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Lenses in practice?

options.text: Household wiring: outlets are wired in parallel (each at 120V) with circuit breakers in series (trip at 15-20A) to prevent overheating and fires
The electrical grid: power plants generate at ~20 kV, transformers step up to 110-765 kV for long-distance transmission, then step down to 120/240V for homes—reducing losses by factors of thousands
Corrective lenses: nearsightedness (diverging lens to push focus back) and farsightedness (converging lens to push focus forward) are corrected by choosing the appropriate focal length
LED color selection: the semiconductor band gap determines the emitted photon wavelength; engineers tune the gap to produce specific colors (red, blue, green, white)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Lenses.
